skill.name: feature-file
skill.description: Manage a persistent features.yml file for requirements, phases, tests, known issues, decisions, and version tracking.
skill.summary: Track project requirements and implementation progress in a durable features.yml file.
skill.author: Chemiseblanc

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are Markdown backticks, YAML examples, or security vocabulary in documentation. The confirmed risks are executable helper scripts, local filesystem access to features.yml, and fixed git subprocess calls in the version checker.

medium_findings.title: Fixed Git Subprocess Calls in Version Checker
medium_findings.description: The version checker invokes git through subprocess.run. The commands use argument lists, fixed subcommands, parsed line numbers, and commit hashes, so command injection was not confirmed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external command pattern is real and runs local git commands. Confidence is below very high because the implementation avoids shell=True and constrains dynamic values.

medium_findings.title: Executable Helper Scripts With Inline Dependencies
medium_findings.description: The skill includes executable uv scripts that read project-local features.yml and declare PyYAML. This is legitimate for the skill, but users should review scripts before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts are clearly executable and part of normal operation. The risk is contextual because the code uses safe YAML parsing and does not show exfiltration or destructive behavior.

low_findings.title: Documentation Examples Triggered Command and Crypto Heuristics
low_findings.description: Many static findings are Markdown code spans, YAML examples, and schema documentation. Mentions of bcrypt, JWT, OAuth, and tokens are example content, not implemented cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is inside Markdown examples and documentation. I found no executable crypto implementation in these locations.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Several script findings are Markdown formatting in generated output. They wrap status values, paths, and commit identifiers for display only.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These lines build Markdown strings and do not invoke a shell. The surrounding functions only format already parsed data.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance and Key Heuristics Were False Positives
low_findings.description: Scanner hits for reconnaissance and key material map to schema examples, status text, and a sort key. No secret file access or host probing was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are ordinary documentation and Python sorting logic. I found no code that reads certificate files, private keys, or system inventory.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run Used for Git History Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: scripts/check-version.py runs git commands to inspect repository state and blame features.yml. Calls use argv lists without shell=True, reducing injection risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess pattern is present and executable. The risk is moderated by fixed git arguments and lack of shell expansion.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local YAML File Parsing
dangerous_patterns.description: Helper scripts parse features.yml from the current directory. They use yaml.safe_load_all, which avoids unsafe object construction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The local file parsing behavior is explicit and central to the skill. Safe parsing prevents the common PyYAML code execution issue.

content.value_statement: Teams lose project intent when requirements live only in chat or tasks. This skill keeps feature scope, tests, decisions, and progress in a persistent features.yml file.
actual_capabilities: Creates or updates a features.yml planning document when requested.
Tracks feature phase, requirements, decisions, known issues, and changelog entries.
Links requirements to test cases through tested-by references.
Reports incomplete requirements, known issues, validation errors, and test status.
Checks git history to recommend version bumps for changed feature sections.
Supports plain text, Markdown, and JSON output in helper scripts.
limitations: It only works with the features.yml format described by the skill.
It does not replace normal issue tracking or release management tools.
Version checks require a git repository with features.yml tracked.
Helper scripts must be reviewed before running in sensitive repositories.

use_cases.title: Plan a new feature before coding
use_cases.description: Capture requirements, expected tests, decisions, and initial phase before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Audit delivery progress across features
use_cases.description: Summarize incomplete requirements, failing tests, and phase blockers from one persistent YAML file.

use_cases.title: Preserve rationale during AI-assisted work
use_cases.description: Keep decisions and known issues close to the code so future sessions can recover project context.

prompt_templates.title: Create a feature file
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a features.yml file for this project. Include one feature, clear requirements, an initial changelog, and planned test cases.

prompt_templates.title: Update progress after implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Update features.yml for the requirement I just completed. Mark related tests, record any known issues, and keep the changelog current.

prompt_templates.title: Find remaining work
prompt_templates.prompt: Review features.yml and summarize requirements that are not complete. Group the work by feature and identify missing tests.

prompt_templates.title: Audit feature readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate features.yml against the phase rules. Identify blockers for Complete status and recommend any needed version bump.

output_examples.input: Ask for a new checkout feature plan.
output_examples.output: A features.yml entry with phase, version, changelog, requirements, decisions, and planned tests for checkout behavior.

output: A feature-by-feature list of incomplete requirements.
Each item includes status, description, and linked tests when available.

output_examples.input: Ask whether a feature can move to Complete.
output_examples.output: A readiness summary showing incomplete requirements, failing tests, missing decisions, and version bump recommendations.

best_practices: Update features.yml before and after each meaningful implementation step.
Use stable requirement IDs so tests and history stay traceable.
Run validation before committing feature or test status changes.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill proactively when no features.yml file exists.
Do not mark requirements Complete before linked tests are updated.
Do not use features.yml as the only source for product prioritization.

faq.question: When should this skill activate?
faq.answer: Use it automatically when features.yml already exists. Use it manually when you want to create that file.

faq.question: Does it write application code?
faq.answer: No. It manages planning, progress, and validation data that supports implementation work.

faq.question: Can it track tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It records test names, files, descriptions, pass status, and links from requirements to tests.

faq.question: Does the version checker require git?
faq.answer: Yes. The version checker inspects git history and requires features.yml to be tracked.

faq.question: Is the YAML parser unsafe?
faq.answer: The scripts use yaml.safe_load_all, which avoids unsafe PyYAML object construction.

faq.question: Can teams use multiple feature documents?
faq.answer: The documented workflow expects one multi-document features.yml file in the project root.
